title: The Miranda Hypothesis: How Hamilton Poisoned Persona Evals - Jacob E. Thomas, Results Gen
summary: Jacob E. Thomas presents the Miranda Hypothesis, which identifies how cultural representation density in training data causes persona-based AI systems to output smoothed cultural composites rather than reasoning from primary sources. He proposes a shift from cognitive simulation benchmarks to epistemic simulation with temporally-anchored, expert-evaluated rubrics that detect anachronistic reasoning and documentary inconsistencies that automated metrics miss.
writeup: Jacob E. Thomas introduces the Miranda Hypothesis: when training data carries dense cultural representation of a figure or period, persona-based systems produce smoothed cultural composites instead of reasoning from primary sources. The talk frames this as Miranda distortion, a failure mode that standard persona prompts and automated metrics fail to catch. Thomas argues for moving from cognitive simulation benchmarks to epistemic simulation, using temporally-anchored, expert-evaluated rubrics that surface anachronistic reasoning and documentary inconsistencies.
